prompt: I put a counter inside Arc<Cell<usize>> so multiple threads can share it, but the compiler says Cell<usize> cannot be shared between threads safely.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `Cell<usize>` cannot be shared between threads safely
 --> src/main.rs:10:9
  |
10|         thread::spawn(move || {
  |         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ `Cell<usize>` cannot be shared between threads safely
  |
  = help: within `Arc<Cell<usize>>`, the trait `Sync` is not implemented for `Cell<usize>`
  = note: required because it appears within the type `Arc<Cell<usize>>`
  = note: required by a bound in `thread::spawn`
explanation: Cell<T> provides interior mutability through single-threaded exclusive access — it is Send (you can move it to another thread) but not Sync (you cannot share a &Cell<T> between threads). Arc<T> shares a &T across threads, so it requires T: Sync. The solution is AtomicUsize, which wraps a usize and performs all reads and writes as atomic hardware instructions. fetch_add atomically increments and returns the old value; Ordering::Relaxed is sufficient for a counter that doesn't synchronise other memory accesses. Unlike Cell, AtomicUsize is both Send and Sync.

prompt: I'm sharing a flag across threads using Arc<RefCell<bool>>, but the compiler says RefCell<bool> cannot be shared between threads safely. I need threads to read and write a boolean flag.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `RefCell<bool>` cannot be shared between threads safely
 --> src/main.rs:9:19
  |
9 |     let worker = thread::spawn(move || {
  |                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ `RefCell<bool>` cannot be shared between threads safely
  |
  = help: within `Arc<RefCell<bool>>`, the trait `Sync` is not implemented for `RefCell<bool>`
  = note: required because it appears within the type `Arc<RefCell<bool>>`
  = note: required by a bound in `thread::spawn`
explanation: RefCell<T> uses a runtime borrow counter to enforce exclusivity, but that counter is not protected by any synchronisation mechanism — concurrent access from multiple threads would race on the counter itself. The compiler prevents this by not implementing Sync for RefCell. AtomicBool uses a single atomic instruction for each load/store, making it safe to share. For this producer/consumer flag, Ordering::Release on the store and Ordering::Acquire on the load establishes a happens-before relationship: any writes before the Release store are guaranteed to be visible after the Acquire load, which matters when the flag is used to signal completion of other work.

prompt: I'm implementing a spinlock using AtomicBool. My implementation seems correct but I used Relaxed ordering everywhere and my lock doesn't actually protect the critical section on multi-core hardware.
explanation: Relaxed ordering only guarantees atomicity of the individual operation — it makes no promises about ordering relative to other memory accesses. A spinlock must use Acquire on lock acquisition and Release on unlock to establish a happens-before edge: the thread that calls lock() will see all writes made by the previous lock holder before its unlock(). Acquire prevents reads/writes in the critical section from being moved before the CAS, and Release prevents them from being moved after the store. The optimisation of spinning on a Relaxed load before retrying the CAS reduces cache-line invalidation traffic on contended locks.

prompt: I want to atomically update a shared value only if it hasn't changed since I last read it (a compare-and-swap). My loop with compare_exchange never makes progress because I'm not handling the failure case correctly.
explanation: compare_exchange_weak is preferred over compare_exchange inside a retry loop for two reasons: (1) on LL/SC architectures it is a single instruction and avoids the retry the hardware already does inside compare_exchange; (2) the name communicates to readers that spurious failures are expected. Crucially, on failure compare_exchange_weak returns Err(current) — the actual current value of the atomic. Assigning `old = current` avoids the extra load on the next iteration and prevents a live-loop in pathological cases. Extracting `old` before the loop also avoids a redundant load on the first iteration.

prompt: I'm building a lazy initializer: the first call computes an expensive value and subsequent calls return it. I used AtomicPtr but my code has a double-free if two threads race during initialisation.
explanation: The broken AtomicPtr approach has two problems: (1) Relaxed ordering on the success CAS means the String data might not be visible to other threads before they read the pointer; you need Ordering::Release on the store and Ordering::Acquire on the load. (2) The loser thread's `drop(Box::from_raw(new_val))` is correct (avoids a leak) but only if the winner stored its pointer correctly — easy to get wrong under other racing conditions. OnceLock<T> (stable since Rust 1.70) solves all of this: it is designed for exactly-once initialisation, uses correct memory ordering internally, and never double-initialises or leaks even under concurrent access.
